steps.label: A piece
steps.narrative: Start with just a someone or something. This isn't a sentence yet — it's a piece of one. The reader is left waiting: the dog… what?

subject: Subject: "The dog"
The subject names who or what the sentence will be about. Alone, it's a fragment — a move that hasn't finished happening. It needs a verb.

steps.label: + Verb = sentence
steps.narrative: Add a doing or being word and the move completes. Subject + verb = a sentence kernel — the smallest complete sentence there is.

subject: Subject: "The dog"
Ask: who slept? → The dog. Now the subject has something to do.
verb: The verb finishes the move. Slept needs nothing after it — some verbs are complete on their own. This two-word kernel is a full, legal sentence.

steps.label: + Detail (still one kernel)
steps.narrative: Grow the kernel with detail. The extra words answer which? how? where? — but the skeleton is still just dog slept.

subject: Complete subject: "The old dog"
Old adds detail inside the subject. The simple subject — the kernel's bone — is still dog.
verb: Unchanged. Detail orbits the verb without replacing it.
modifier: Answers where? Remove it and the sentence still stands: The old dog slept. Optional words are modifiers; the kernel is what's left when they go.

steps.label: A piece
steps.narrative: A someone with no doing: the goalie…? The thought hangs.

subject: Subject: "The goalie"
A subject waiting for a verb. Until one arrives, this is a fragment.

steps.label: + Verb = sentence
steps.narrative: One verb turns the fragment into a complete move.

subject: Subject: "The goalie"
Who dove? → The goalie.
verb: A single action verb completes the kernel. Two words, full sentence.

steps.label: + Detail (still one kernel)
steps.narrative: Detail makes it vivid; the kernel stays goalie dove.

subject: Complete subject: "The rookie goalie"
Rookie tells us which goalie. Simple subject: goalie.
verb: The engine of the sentence, unchanged.
modifier: Answers where? Strip it away and the kernel still stands.

steps.label: A piece
steps.narrative: This time the missing half is the someone. A doing with no doer: …crackled? What crackled?

verb: A verb alone is also a fragment — the reader can't picture the move without knowing who or what performs it.

steps.label: + Subject = sentence
steps.narrative: Give the doing a doer and the kernel completes.

subject: Subject: "The fire"
What crackled? → The fire. The kernel is complete.
verb: The verb carries the sound of the sentence. All night is bonus detail answering when?

steps.label: + Detail (still one kernel)
steps.narrative: Grown, but the skeleton is still fire crackled.

subject: Complete subject: "The dying fire"
Dying — a verb form working as a describer — tells us which fire. Simple subject: fire.
modifier: How? Softly. When? All night. Both removable; the kernel survives.

steps.label: S + V
steps.narrative: Start from the kernel: a subject and a verb.

subject: Subject: "The dog"
The subject tells us WHO performs the action. Ask: "Who slept?" → The dog.
verb: The main verb is the engine of the predicate (everything that isn't the subject). Slept is intransitive — it needs no object to be complete.

steps.label: + Direct Obj
steps.narrative: Some verbs are transitive — they pass their action to an object. Switch to chased: chased what?

subject: Subject: "The dog"
Still our performer. Who chased the ball? → The dog.
verb: Transitive — it transfers action to an object. Unlike slept, chased demands something to chase; the sentence would feel unfinished without it.
do: The DO receives the action. Ask: "Chased what?" → the ball. Passive test: "The ball was chased" ✓ — so it's a DO.

steps.label: + Indirect Obj
steps.narrative: Insert an indirect object — who the ball is being fetched for.

subject: Subject: "The dog"
Who fetched? → The dog.
verb: Swapped to "fetched" — a verb that naturally hands something to someone, so it can take both an IO and a DO.
io: The IO tells us for whom the action is done. "Fetched for whom?" → its owner. Rephrase test: "fetched the ball for its owner."
do: Still the thing being fetched. The IO sits between the verb and the DO.

steps.label: + Modifiers
steps.narrative: Modifiers add detail — when, where, how, which one. They're optional; remove them and the core sentence still stands.

subject: Subject: "The loyal dog"
"Loyal" is an adjective modifier inside the complete subject. The simple subject is still "dog."
verb: Unchanged. Modifiers orbit the verb but don't change what it is.
modifier: Three modifiers, three jobs: "loyal" (adjective — which dog), "eagerly" (adverb — how), "at the park" (prepositional phrase — where). Remove all three: "The dog fetched its owner the ball" still works.

steps.label: S + V
steps.narrative: Core kernel: subject and verb.

subject: Subject: "Mrs. Reyes"
A proper noun as subject. Who explained? → Mrs. Reyes.
verb: An action verb. Standing alone it feels open — explained what? This verb wants an object.

steps.label: + Direct Obj
steps.narrative: Add a direct object — explained what?

subject: Subject: "Mrs. Reyes"
Who explained? → Mrs. Reyes.
verb: Now transitive — transferring action to "the lesson."
do: Receives the action. Explained what? → the lesson.

steps.label: + Indirect Obj
steps.narrative: To add a receiver, we swap verbs: English says taught the students the lesson but never explained the students the lesson — only some verbs accept an indirect object. (With explain, the receiver needs a phrase: explained the lesson to the students.)

subject: Subject: "Mrs. Reyes"
Who taught?
verb: Teach belongs to the give-hand-tell family of verbs that can take a receiver directly. Explain doesn't — a real demand each verb makes. When a verb refuses an IO, use a prepositional phrase instead.
io: Taught to whom? → the students. Rephrase test: "taught the lesson to the students."

steps.label: + Modifiers
steps.narrative: Layer in modifiers for detail.

subject: Subject: "Mrs. Reyes"
Unchanged.
io: "Confused" is a modifier inside the IO phrase. The IO still answers to whom?
do: "Difficult" modifies "lesson," but the whole phrase is still the DO.

steps.label: S + Linking V (not done yet)
steps.narrative: Linking verbs don't show action — they promise a description. "The sky looks…" leaves the reader waiting for the payoff: looks how?

subject: Subject: "The sky"
What looks? → The sky.
verb: "Looks" here doesn't mean the sky has eyes — it connects the subject to a coming description. A linking verb makes a promise the sentence must keep: it demands a complement. Others: is, seems, becomes, appears, feels, tastes, sounds.

steps.label: + Pred. Adjective
steps.narrative: Keep the promise with a predicate adjective — an adjective that describes the subject across the linking verb.

subject: Subject: "The sky"
What looks electric? → The sky.
verb: Connects subject → description. No action, no object.
pa: A predicate adjective is a subject complement that uses an adjective to describe the subject through a linking verb. The sky = electric. Compare a predicate nominative, which uses a noun to rename the subject.

steps.label: + Modifiers
steps.narrative: Add modifiers for richer description.

subject: Subject: "The evening sky"
"Evening" modifies "sky" inside the complete subject.
pa: Describes the subject. "Absolutely" intensifies it but doesn't change its grammatical job.

steps.label: S + Linking V (not done yet)
steps.narrative: Subject + linking verb — the promise again. "She is…" — is what?

subject: Subject: "She"
A pronoun as subject. Who is? → She.
verb: The most common linking verb. It connects but shows no action, and it leaves the sentence hungry for a complement.

steps.label: + Pred. Nominative
steps.narrative: A predicate nominative is a noun that renames the subject. She = pilot.

subject: Subject: "She"
Who is a pilot? → She.
verb: Connects the subject to a renaming noun.
pn: A noun that renames the subject through a linking verb. She = a pilot. This is NOT a direct object — nothing is acted upon. Compare: "She called a pilot" (DO — she ≠ pilot, action verb) vs. "She is a pilot" (PN — she = pilot, linking verb).

steps.label: + Modifiers
steps.narrative: Layer in modifiers.

subject: Subject: "She"
Unchanged.
pn: "Licensed" modifies "pilot" inside the PN phrase, but the whole noun phrase renames "she."

steps.label: S + V
steps.narrative: Subject + action verb — and this verb is hungry.

subject: Subject: "The team"
Who elected? → The team.
verb: An action verb that demands an object — elected whom? Without one, the move is unfinished.

steps.label: + Direct Obj
steps.narrative: Elected whom? Add a direct object.

steps.label: + Obj. Complement
steps.narrative: An object complement renames or describes the direct object (not the subject).

subject: Subject: "The team"
Who elected?
verb: Takes the pattern S + V + DO + OC.
oc: Renames or describes the DIRECT OBJECT. Jordan = captain. Compare: a subject complement describes the subject via a linking verb. Here "elected" is an action verb — that's the key difference.

steps.label: + Modifiers
steps.narrative: Polish with modifiers.

subject: Subject: "The varsity team"
"Varsity" modifies "team."
modifier: "Varsity" (adjective — which team), "unanimously" (adverb — how), "last Friday" (noun phrase working as an adverb — when).

steps.label: S + V
steps.narrative: Subject + verb.

subject: Subject: "Grandma"
Who baked? → Grandma.
verb: An action verb, complete here on its own — but able to take an object.

steps.label: + Direct Obj
steps.narrative: Baked what? Add a direct object.

steps.label: + Indirect Obj
steps.narrative: Baked for whom? Add an indirect object.

subject: Subject: "Grandma"
Who baked?
verb: Bake, like give and hand, happily takes a receiver: IO + DO.
io: Baked for whom? → us. Rephrase test: "baked a pie for us."

steps.label: + Modifiers
steps.narrative: Finish with modifiers.

steps.label: Participle
steps.narrative: A participle is a verb form acting as an adjective. Present participles end in -ing; past participles usually end in -d/-ed but are often irregular (broken, sung, written).

v1.body:
"shattered" — past participle of shatter. It doesn't work as the sentence's verb (that's lay); it modifies vase, answering which vase? That's an adjective's job.

Test: could you replace it with an ordinary adjective like broken or red? → "The red vase lay on the floor." ✓ Adjective slot confirmed.

steps.label: Gerund
steps.narrative: A gerund is an -ing verb form acting as a noun. It names an activity — and takes a noun's slot in the sentence.

v1.body:
"Reading" — the -ing form of read, but here it names an activity instead of performing it. The sentence's verb is relaxes; reading is what relaxes her — the subject.

Test: can you replace it with "it"? → "It relaxes her." ✓ Noun confirmed.

steps.label: Infinitive
steps.narrative: An infinitive is to + a base verb. It can act as a noun, adjective, or adverb — the most versatile verbal.

v1.body:
"to graduate" — to + base verb. Here it renames goal after the linking verb is. Goal = to graduate. That's a noun job (predicate nominative).

Don't confuse: "to" + noun (to the store) is a prepositional phrase. "to" + verb (to graduate) is an infinitive.

steps.label: Participle
steps.narrative: A participle is a verb form acting as an adjective. It describes a noun.

v1.body:
"whispering" — present participle of whisper. The sentence's verb is moved; this word describes wind. What kind of wind? A whispering one. Adjective work.

Notice how much more vivid whispering wind is than soft wind. Participles carry a verb's energy into description.

steps.label: Gerund
steps.narrative: A gerund is an -ing verb form acting as a noun.

v1.body:
"swimming" — here the -ing word names an activity. Loves what? → swimming. It's the direct object of loves, doing a noun's job.

Watch closely: "She is swimming" uses swimming as part of the verb (present progressive). Same form, entirely different job. Context decides everything.

steps.label: Infinitive
steps.narrative: An infinitive is to + a base verb — the most flexible verbal.

v1.body:
"to win" — to + base verb. It answers why did he train hard? To win. That's an adverb job (purpose), modifying trained.

If you can substitute "in order to" without awkwardness ("he trained hard in order to win"), it's an adverb of purpose.

steps.label: Participial Phrase
steps.narrative: A participial phrase = participle + objects + modifiers. The whole phrase acts as an adjective.

v1.body:
"Exhausted from the long climb" — led by the past participle exhausted, which pulls along a prepositional phrase (from the long climb) telling why. The whole phrase describes hikers.

Keep it attached: a participial phrase at the start of a sentence describes the subject that follows. "Exhausted from the long climb, the hikers…" ✓ — it's the hikers who are exhausted, just as the grammar promises.

steps.label: Gerund Phrase
steps.narrative: A gerund phrase = gerund + objects + modifiers. The whole phrase acts as a noun.

v1.body:
"Running a marathon" — led by the gerund running, which takes a marathon as its own object (gerunds keep some verb powers even while working as nouns). The whole phrase is the subject of requires.

Test: "It requires months of training." ✓ Noun confirmed.

steps.label: Infinitive Phrase
steps.narrative: An infinitive phrase = infinitive + objects + modifiers. The whole phrase can act as noun, adjective, or adverb.

v1.body:
"to finish the project" — infinitive to finish + direct object the project. Answers why did she stay late? — an adverb modifying stayed.

Infinitive phrases can also be nouns ("To forgive is divine") or adjectives ("the key to open the door"). Check the job, not the shape.

steps.label: Participial Phrase
steps.narrative: A participial phrase = participle + objects + modifiers. The whole phrase acts as an adjective.

v1.body:
"wearing a red jacket" — led by participle wearing, which takes its own direct object a red jacket. The phrase sits right next to boy and modifies it: which boy? The one wearing a red jacket.

When the phrase is essential to identify the noun, no commas. When it's extra information, commas: "My brother, wearing a red jacket, waved at us."

steps.label: Gerund Phrase
steps.narrative: A gerund phrase = gerund + objects + modifiers. The whole phrase acts as a noun.

v1.body:
"hiking in the mountains" — gerund hiking + a prepositional phrase modifying it. Enjoy what? → hiking in the mountains. That's a direct object — a noun's job.

Gerund phrases are quiet workhorses: one construction names an entire activity without needing a new clause.

steps.label: Infinitive Phrase
steps.narrative: An infinitive phrase can act as noun, adjective, or adverb.

v1.body:
"to help me move" — modifies someone. Which someone? Someone to help me move. That reading treats the infinitive as an adjective.

Worth knowing: many grammarians also read this phrase as an adverb of purpose (why do I need someone?). Both analyses appear in major reference grammars; the ambiguity is genuine. Compare the clear noun use: "I need to sleep" (direct object).

steps.label: Prepositional
steps.narrative: A prepositional phrase starts with a preposition and ends with a noun (the object of the preposition). It acts as an adjective or adverb.

p1.body: "on the windowsill" — preposition on + object the windowsill. It modifies cat (which cat? the one on the windowsill), so it acts as an adjective.

p2.body: "for hours" — preposition for + object hours. It modifies the verb watched (watched how long?), so it acts as an adverb.

steps.label: Appositive
steps.narrative: An appositive phrase is a noun phrase that renames another noun right beside it. It's usually set off by commas.

p1.body:
"a retired firefighter" — a noun phrase that renames my neighbor, packing in extra identification without starting a new sentence. Because it's extra (the sentence works without it), it takes commas.

Essential appositives (needed to know which one) take no commas: "My brother Jake called."

steps.label: Absolute
steps.narrative: An absolute phrase modifies the whole sentence, not just one word. It usually contains a noun + a participle — close to a clause, but missing a finite verb.

p1.body:
"His arms crossed in frustration" — has a noun (arms) and a participle (crossed), but no tensed verb like were crossed. It paints the scene for the whole sentence: how Dad stood while he waited.

Absolutes are always set off with commas — a favorite of narrative writers for adding a close-up shot without a new clause.

steps.label: Prepositional
steps.narrative: A prepositional phrase starts with a preposition and ends with a noun. It acts as an adjective or adverb.

p1.body: "on the top shelf" — tells us which book, modifying the noun book. That's an adjective job.

p2.body: "to my grandfather" — modifies the verb belongs (belongs to whom?). That's an adverb job.

steps.label: Appositive
steps.narrative: An appositive phrase renames the noun beside it.

p1.body:
"our biology teacher" — renames Ms. Chen. Extra identification, not strictly needed, so it's set off with commas.

Appositives are a favorite tool of good writers: they pack in information without spending a new clause.

steps.label: Absolute
steps.narrative: An absolute phrase (noun + participle) hovers over the whole sentence.

p1.body:
"The storm finally ending" — noun (storm) + participle (ending), no tensed verb. If it said "The storm had ended," it would be a full clause. As written, it sets the circumstances for the whole main clause.

Spotting trick: an absolute could become a clause with one small change to its verb.

steps.label: Independent
steps.narrative: An independent clause has a subject + verb AND expresses a complete thought. It can stand alone as a sentence.

c1.body: "The bell rang." — Subject: The bell. Verb: rang. It expresses a full thought and needs nothing else. This IS a sentence.

steps.label: Dependent (same S+V!)
steps.narrative: Add one word — a subordinating conjunction — and the same clause becomes dependent. It can no longer stand alone.

c1.body: "When the bell rang" — same subject (the bell) and verb (rang), but when is a subordinating conjunction. It makes the reader expect more: When the bell rang… what happened? The clause now depends on an independent clause to finish the thought.

steps.label: Combined
steps.narrative: Attach the dependent clause to an independent one — now you have a complex sentence.

chunks.text: the students raced for the door.

c1.body: "When the bell rang" — answers when did the students race? It acts as an adverb modifying raced, and takes a comma because it comes first.

c2.body: "the students raced for the door" — complete thought, stands alone. This is the sentence's backbone; the dependent clause adds context.

steps.label: Independent
steps.narrative: An independent clause has a subject + verb AND expresses a complete thought.

c1.body: "I finished my homework." — Subject: I. Verb: finished. Full thought. Standalone sentence.

steps.label: Dependent (same S+V!)
steps.narrative: Add one subordinating word and the clause becomes dependent.

c1.body: "After I finished my homework" — the word after subordinates the clause: After I finished my homework… then what? The thought is left hanging. That's the mark of a dependent clause.

steps.label: Combined
steps.narrative: Pair it with an independent clause to make a complex sentence.

c1.body: "After I finished my homework" — answers when did I watch? An adverb clause of time, modifying watched.

c2.body: "I watched a movie" — full thought; would be a sentence on its own.

steps.label: Simple
steps.narrative: Simple sentence = one independent clause. No connectors, no dependent clauses.

steps.label: Compound
steps.narrative: Compound sentence = two (or more) independent clauses joined by a comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS) or a semicolon. The two ideas ride as equals.

steps.label: Complex
steps.narrative: Complex sentence = one independent clause + one (or more) dependent clauses. The dependent clause becomes background; the main clause becomes the headline.

steps.label: Compound-Complex
steps.narrative: Compound-complex sentence = at least two independent clauses AND at least one dependent clause. The heavyweight of sentence structures.

steps.label: Noun Clause
steps.narrative: A noun clause acts like a noun — subject, direct object, predicate nominative, or object of a preposition. It usually starts with that, what, who, whoever, whether, if, why, how…

chunks.text: that honesty is the best policy.

c1.body:
"that honesty is the best policy" — a full mini-sentence inside the sentence (subject honesty, verb is). The whole clause answers "She believes what?" — the direct object, a noun's job.

Test: replace the clause with "it" → "She believes it." ✓

steps.label: Adjective Clause
steps.narrative: An adjective clause acts like an adjective — it modifies a noun. It usually starts with a relative pronoun: who, whom, whose, which, that.

chunks.text: who sits in the front row

c1.body:
"who sits in the front row" — the relative pronoun who refers back to student and serves as the clause's subject; its verb is sits. The whole clause tells us which student — an adjective job.

Adjective clauses come right after the noun they modify. Also called relative clauses.

steps.label: Adverb Clause
steps.narrative: An adverb clause acts like an adverb — it modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb. It starts with a subordinating conjunction: because, when, although, if, since, while, until, so that…

c1.body:
"because it was raining" — subject it, verb was raining. The subordinator because answers "cancelled why?" — an adverb job (reason), modifying cancelled.

Adverb clauses can move: "Because it was raining, we cancelled the picnic." (Comma when it leads.)

steps.label: Noun Clause
steps.narrative: A noun clause acts like a noun — it fills a noun slot.

c1.body:
"What he said" — subject of the clause: he; verb: said. The whole clause is the subject of surprised. What surprised everyone? → What he said.

Test: "It surprised everyone." ✓ — noun function confirmed.

steps.label: Adjective Clause
steps.narrative: An adjective clause modifies a noun and usually starts with a relative pronoun.

c1.body:
"that I borrowed last week" — the relative pronoun that refers to book; subject I, verb borrowed. The clause tells us which book.

Notice: you could drop that — "the book I borrowed last week" — and it still works. English allows that when the relative pronoun is an object inside its clause. Hemingway does exactly this: "the dust they raised."

steps.label: Adverb Clause
steps.narrative: An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb, and starts with a subordinating conjunction.

c1.body:
"Although she was tired" — subject she, verb was. The subordinator although sets up a contrast with finished — an adverb clause of concession (a surprising turn).

Because the clause leads the sentence, a comma follows it.

compares.label: “running”

a.label: Verbal
a.why: Here running is a participle (verbal acting as adjective). It describes water — what kind of water? The running kind. The sentence's verb is froze.

b.label: Main Verb
b.why: Here is running is the main verb — present progressive (is + participle). It tells us what the water is doing right now. This is the sentence's verb, not a verbal.

compares.test: The test: Is there a helping verb (is, was, has been) next to the -ing word? If yes, it's probably part of the main verb. If the -ing word stands on its own and modifies a noun or names a thing, it's a verbal.

compares.label: “cooking”

a.label: Verbal
a.why: Here Cooking is a gerund (verbal acting as noun). It's the subject of is — it names an activity. The sentence's verb is is.

b.label: Main Verb
b.why: Here is cooking is the main verb — present progressive. It tells us what the sister is doing. Not a verbal.

compares.test: The test: Drop the helping verb. Does the -ing word still name the activity, or is it left hanging? "Cooking is my favorite hobby" → works standalone. "My sister cooking dinner" → broken; it needs is. That's the tell.

compares.label: “to eat”

a.label: Verbal
a.why: Here to eat is an infinitive (verbal acting as adverb). It answers why did we stop? The main verb is stopped; the infinitive adds purpose.

b.label: Main Verb
b.why: Here ate is the main verb — conjugated for tense and subject. A verb that shows tense and agrees with a subject is the sentence's verb, not an infinitive.

compares.test: The test: Can you change the word's tense? Main verbs can: ate, eats, will eat. Infinitives can't — to eat stays to eat whether the sentence is past, present, or future. The invariability is a dead giveaway.

compares.label: “broken”

a.label: Verbal
a.why: Here broken is a participle (verbal acting as adjective). It describes chair. The sentence's verb is sat.

b.label: Main Verb
b.why: Here was broken is the main verb — a passive form (was + past participle) describing what happened to the chair. The helping verb was is the tell.

compares.test: The test: Past participles (-ed / irregular) look identical whether they're verbals or part of a passive verb. The difference: does a helping verb come with it? No helper = participle. Helper = main verb.

compares.label: tired vs. finished

a.label: Version A
a.why: The main clause — the headline — is she finished the race. Tiredness is backgrounded inside the although clause. This version celebrates the finish.

b.label: Version B
b.why: Same two facts, flipped hierarchy. Now finishing is background and exhaustion is the headline. This version worries about her.

compares.test: The move: Subordination decides which fact is the story and which is the setting. Before you combine two sentences, ask: which one deserves the main clause?

compares.label: equal vs. ranked

a.label: Coordinated
a.why: And presents the two events as equals — a flat sequence, one thing after another. Calm, reportorial.

b.label: Subordinated
b.why: When demotes the outage to a time-stamp and promotes the candle-lighting to the main event. The sentence is now about the response, not the outage.

compares.test: The move: Coordination (and, but, so) says "these ideas are partners." Subordination (when, because, although) says "this idea serves that one." Choose the relationship you mean.
